title: ComfyUI for Image Manipulation: Remove BG, Combine Images, Adjust Colors (Ep08)
description:
Learn how to manipulate images inside ComfyUI using nodes. In this tutorial, you will see how to remove backgrounds, combine images like layers, apply color adjustments and filters, and automate image editing workflows. Instead of using traditional tools like Photoshop, this approach uses node-based workflows that allow you to automate multiple editing steps and even integrate AI image editing models.

By the end of this episode, you will understand how to build workflows that automatically remove backgrounds, place objects on new backgrounds, apply visual effects, and run AI editing models to enhance the final image. This makes ComfyUI a powerful solution for product images, creative edits, and automated image pipelines.

What You’ll Learn

• How to remove image backgrounds using different ComfyUI nodes
• How to batch remove backgrounds from an entire folder of images
• How to combine foreground and background images like layers
• How to position, scale, rotate, and transform images
•

Let's say you have a product like this bottle.  You can use one node to remove the background,  then use another node to add a different background behind it.  Then you can use AI to edit that image,  maybe add shadows in a cap,  and then expand the workflow to add a label.  At the end, you can add some nodes for filters or color correction,  maybe add some noise, and much more.  The good part is that you can change the product,  and with a single run, you get all those steps automatically. Motion blur,  maybe you need to blur a background for a card to show it in motion.  Channel Shake adds this color shift effect,  like chromatic aberration.  Half-tone is that old print effect.  Maybe you can do some comics and apply it as an overlay on top of another image.  Invert image just inverts the colors.  You can adjust the brightness and contrast how you want,  just like in many photo editing software programs.  Auto-adjusts can do a subtle adjustment,  but it also has more options you can use to adjust the colors how you want  and create different effects. So we connected all those effect nodes,  one after the other,  to create something like in this example.  So now I have this filter effect  that looks pretty interesting,  isn't it? Now, because it is not the same size  or even the same ratio,  some things will not work.  I guess you can add some extra nodes to make the size match,  or adjust it here from resize to always be a certain size,  but let me show you what happens when the sizes are different. So what I can do to fix it is scale the paper texture,  so you can see I can make it larger.  So let me adjust it a little bit more so it fits.  So now it looks great.  A transformation from a clean one to a dirtier, older look.  So only your imagination is the limit of what workflows you can make.  Play with nodes, change settings, break things, and start over,  because that is the way you learn comfy UI,  and you can have some fun in the process. Aspect ratio lets you stretch the image,  maybe not on characters,  but it might be useful on some objects. This time it is about image scale,  since many AI models work with a certain size,  so you might want at some point in the workflow  to adjust the size of the image to fit the AI workflow. That is very useful.  For example, you want to generate a new image,  but it should be the same size as the original. Another useful one is when you want your image  to be a maximum certain size.  In this case, the largest size I want it to be is this,  so you can see it keeps that size  and keeps the ratio of the original image. So if I want it scaled two times,  I get what I asked for, a bigger image. Most of the models we have now  are trained at around one megapixel anyway.  Then for the last node,  you have more options for control.  You can set the exact width and height you want.  You also have more options for how to keep proportions,  like I usually use crop,  but sometimes maybe for out paint or something,  you do not want to crop.  You want the entire image and add some extra pixels to fill that.  So play with those settings and see what each one can do.  You can also choose on what device to run,  like CPU or GPU.  On the GPU, the Lonso's method I used is not supported,  but if I use something else like nearest exact,  it should work just fine.  But you can leave it on CPU by default to save some VRAM,  but I assume it does not consume too much anyway.  And that is for the scale node.  Let's move to the next workflow, the Lama Remover. So I mask this area, and then I click on the Save button.  Now we have that mask there, so when I run the workflow,  we get that thing removed.  Now it is very fast, so it is not as good as an edit model,  but you get control over the things you remove.  So it might look smudge sometimes,  but it is helpful in many situations.  And you have these settings to try until you get the results you are after.  And if you want to edit it, you can go back to the Mask Editor.  You can clean the selection if you want, or you can mask more things.  Let's say I do not want this button, and maybe this thing here.  Save the mask, so now we can see that mask there, run the workflow,  and now we have our image without those elements.  It is not perfect, but maybe with some different settings or a different mask,  you can improve it.  You can also use it to remove watermarks and other things.  And for the speed, if we look in the console,  it takes about two seconds, so it is quite fast.  Let's move now to the last workflow,  where I combined different nodes that we learned today with a flux-cline workflow from Episode 4.  I loaded the background image here, then I added this image of an empty bottle,  then I removed the background from the bottle,  and I used this image blend node to merge them together.  So when I run it, I get that bottle on the exact same background.  So simple stuff, one layer on top of the other, just like in Photoshop.  Then I added a flux-cline workflow in this group,  and when I am happy with the bottle position and scale and so on,  I can enable this group.
